formulas.name: Content
formulas.formula:
Here's the complete formula sheet for CBSE Class 11 Accountancy - Financial Statements II:

```json
[
  {
    "name": "Trading Profit",
    "formula": "Trading Profit = Cost of Goods Sold - Opening Stock + Closing Stock",
    "variables": "Cost of Goods Sold: Total Cost of Goods Sold during the accounting period, Opening Stock: Stock at the beginning of the accounting period, Closing Stock: Stock at the end of the accounting period",
    "units": "",
    "notes": "Trading Profit is the profit earned from the sale of goods and is calculated by adding the closing stock and subtracting the opening stock from the cost of goods sold."
  },
  {
    "name": "Gross Profit",
    "formula": "Gross Profit = Trading Profit + Other Income",
    "variables": "Trading Profit: Profit earned from the sale of goods, Other Income: Income earned from other sources such as interest, dividends, etc.",
    "units": "",
    "notes": "Gross Profit is the profit earned from the sale of goods and other income."
  },
  {
    "name": "Gross Profit Ratio",
    "formula": "Gross Profit Ratio = Gross Profit / Total Revenue x 100",
    "variables": "Gross Profit: Profit earned from the sale of goods and other income, Total Revenue: Total revenue earned from sales and other sources.",
    "units": "%",
    "notes": "Gross Profit Ratio is the ratio of gross profit to total revenue and is expressed as a percentage."
  },
  {
    "name": "Operating Profit",
    "formula": "Operating Profit = Gross Profit - Operating Expenses",
    "variables": "Gross Profit: Profit earned from the sale of goods and other income, Operating Expenses: Expenses incurred in the day-to-day operations of the business.",
    "units": "",
    "notes": "Operating Profit is the profit earned from the sale of goods and other income after deducting operating expenses."
  },
  {
    "name": "Operating Profit Ratio",
    "formula": "Operating Profit Ratio = Operating Profit / Total Revenue x 100",
    "variables": "Operating Profit: Profit earned from the sale of goods and other income after deducting operating expenses, Total Revenue: Total revenue earned from sales and other sources.",
    "units": "%",
    "notes": "Operating Profit Ratio is the ratio of operating profit to total revenue and is expressed as a percentage."
  },
  {
    "name": "Net Profit",
    "formula": "Net Profit = Operating Profit + Non-Operating Income - Non-Operating Expenses",
    "variables": "Operating Profit: Profit earned from the sale of goods and other income after deducting operating expenses, Non-Operating Income: Income earned from non-operating sources such as interest, dividends, etc., Non-Operating Expenses: Expenses incurred from non-operating sources.",
    "units": "",
    "notes": "Net Profit is the profit earned by the business after deducting all expenses, including operating and non-operating expenses."
  },
  {
    "name": "Net Profit Ratio",
    "formula": "Net Profit Ratio = Net Profit / Total Revenue x 100",
    "variables": "Net Profit: Profit earned by the business after deducting all expenses, Total Revenue: Total revenue earned from sales and other sources.",
    "units": "%",
    "notes": "Net Profit Ratio is the ratio of net profit to total revenue and is expressed as a percentage."
  },
  {
    "name": "Financial Statement Formula",
    "formula": "Assets = Liabilities + Capital",
    "variables": "Assets: Total assets of the business, Liabilities: Total liabilities of the business, Capital: Total capital invested in the business.",
    "units": "",
    "notes": "This formula is the basis of the accounting equation and represents the relationship between assets, liabilities, and capital."
  }
]
```

Please note that the "Financial Statement Formula" is not a mathematical formula, but a fundamental accounting equation that represents the relationship between assets, liabilities, and capital.
